statement:
My research focuses on making deep learning models more reliable in open-world conditions — specifically under adversarial perturbations and distribution shift. I work at the intersection of uncertainty estimation, adversarial robustness, and computer vision, with the goal of building ML systems that know what they don't know.

Currently, I am exploring Monte Carlo Dropout and conformal prediction as post-hoc uncertainty quantification methods for object detection pipelines. The core question: can we flag adversarial or out-of-distribution inputs in real-time without retraining the detector?

Longer term, I am interested in calibration under distribution shift, reliable uncertainty propagation in multi-stage pipelines, and applications of these methods to safety-critical systems.

interests.description: Studying how neural networks fail under adversarial perturbations and developing detection and mitigation strategies for object detection pipelines.
Applying conformal prediction and Monte Carlo Dropout to quantify predictive uncertainty in deep learning models, with a focus on out-of-distribution detection.
Building and evaluating object detection and image classification systems, with an emphasis on robustness and calibration in real-world deployment settings.
